name: Lituya Bay
short_description: Remote coastal rockhounding in Southeast Alaska's pristine wilderness. Search glacial beaches and tidal zones for jade, quartz, and feldspar specimens among dramatic mountain scenery.
description:
Lituya Bay is one of Alaska's most remote and geologically dramatic rockhounding destinations, accessible only by boat or floatplane from Juneau. The bay sits at the terminus of tidewater glaciers that continuously deposit fresh mineral material onto its beaches and tidal flats. The collecting area spans several miles of accessible shoreline where waves, tidal action, and glacial melt expose jade, quartz, feldspar, and other minerals from the surrounding Coast Range bedrock.

The geology here reflects the Coastal Ranges' collision-zone history. Metamorphic rocks including jade nephrite, serpentine, and granitic feldspars were thrust upward during tectonic activity over the past 100 million years. Ongoing glacial erosion continuously exposes these minerals, making the beaches a natural sorting system where heavier specimens accumulate in beach deposits.

Collecting at Lituya Bay requires serious wilderness preparation. There are no established facilities, no maintained trails, and access is controlled by weather, tidal windows, and logistics costs. Most collectors visit as part of guided Alaska rockhounding tours or charter boat expeditions. The bay's extreme remoteness means you must be self-sufficient for water, food, and emergency response.

The tidal range here exceeds 20 feet, creating excellent collecting opportunities during low tide when fresh material is exposed. Specimens typically range from small pebbles to hand-sized pieces. Jade here often shows green, white, or mottled coloration. Quartz occurs as clear to milky crystals, and feldspars display feldspar's characteristic luster and cleavage.

This is not a casual rockhounding spot. Plan expeditions in advance, hire reputable Alaska guides familiar with Lituya Bay's hazards, and bring proper cold-weather gear, safety equipment, and emergency communication devices. The isolation and extreme environment make this a destination for experienced rockhounds and adventurers.
history: Lituya Bay's glaciers and beaches are products of Quaternary glaciation and ongoing tectonic uplift along the Pacific-North American plate boundary. The surrounding Coast Range's metamorphic rocks formed during Mesozoic subduction, and present-day glacial erosion continuously exposes jade nephrite, serpentine, and quartz deposits that have been coveted by indigenous Tlingit peoples and modern collectors for millennia.
tips: Search the lower beach zone during minus tides when maximum fresh material is exposed; jade specimens often concentrate in lag deposits where lighter minerals are winnowed away by wave action. Use a hand lens to identify jade's waxy luster and greenish-white coloration, and check feldspar for its distinctive cleavage planes. Bring heavy-duty buckets for specimens and be prepared to move quickly as tides return; always check tide tables before heading to the beach and maintain awareness of your exit route.
terrain_notes: Lituya Bay sits at sea level (14 ft elevation) with rocky, glacially carved shoreline. Beaches are composed of mixed sand, gravel, and cobbles with frequent large boulders. Tidal flats expose mud and compressed glacial material. Terrain is uneven with sharp rocks and loose material. No trails exist; navigation is by beach walking during tidal windows. The surrounding mountains rise steeply to 5000+ feet. Vegetation is dense coastal rainforest where accessible inland. Cold, wet weather is constant. Footing ranges from difficult to treacherous depending on tide stage and weather.
best_season: June - September. Winter storms and extreme cold make access nearly impossible. Spring (May) and fall (October) can be viable but weather is unpredictable. Summer offers longer daylight, more stable weather windows, and better boat/floatplane availability from Juneau. However, conditions can deteriorate rapidly at any season.
written_directions: Lituya Bay is accessible only by water or air. From Juneau (approximately 70 miles southeast), arrange charter boat service with operators licensed for Southeast Alaska coastal work, or book floatplane service with Juneau-based air charters. Charter operators depart from Juneau's cruise ship docks or private marinas. Travel time by boat is 4-6 hours depending on sea conditions; floatplane takes 45 minutes to 1 hour. No road access exists. Confirm all travel arrangements weeks in advance and monitor marine forecasts. Most rockhounding expeditions are organized through Alaska-based guide companies.
parking_notes: No established parking. Boats anchor in the bay; floatplanes land on the water or designated beaches. Rockhounders typically camp on higher beach areas or return to boats between collecting sessions. Tide stage determines safe beaching locations.
collection_rules: Federal lands and Alaska state waters. Personal use collection of small quantities of minerals and gems is permitted for recreational rockhounding. Do not remove significant geological specimens or fossils. No motorized equipment. Respect any active mining claims (check with Alaska Department of Natural Resources beforehand). Leave no trace; pack out all waste. Comply with all National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration maritime regulations and Alaska boating laws. Firearms should only be carried for wildlife safety and must comply with federal law.
rules: Personal use collection only. No commercial operations or motorized excavation. Respect wildlife and habitat. Pack out all trash. Obey tide schedules and coastal hazard warnings. Carry emergency communication device. Verify no active claims with Alaska DNR before collecting.
nearest_services: Juneau, Alaska, approximately 70 miles northwest. Full services including fuel, food, lodging, boat charter, floatplane operators, and emergency medical. No services at Lituya Bay itself.
accessibility_notes: Extremely difficult terrain. Rocky glacial beach with loose cobbles, boulders, and sharp material. Frequent muddy tidal flats. No wheelchair access or established trails. Footing is treacherous and changes constantly with tide. Requires strong physical fitness, mountaineering experience, and comfort with wilderness navigation. Extreme cold water and hypothermia risk. Not suitable for anyone with limited mobility, young children, or inexperience in remote coastal environments.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Lituya Bay, Alaska?
faq.answer: Lituya Bay is known for jade nephrite, quartz crystals, and feldspar specimens. Jade typically appears in green, white, or mottled colors and is found in beach lag deposits where wave action concentrates heavier minerals. Quartz occurs as clear to milky crystals, and feldspar displays characteristic cleavage and luster. Specimens range from small pebbles to hand-sized pieces.

faq.question: How do I get to Lituya Bay for rockhounding from Juneau?
faq.answer: Lituya Bay is accessible only by boat or floatplane from Juneau, approximately 70 miles away. You must hire a charter boat operator licensed for Southeast Alaska coastal work (4-6 hour trip) or book floatplane service (45 minutes to 1 hour). All travel arrangements must be confirmed weeks in advance. Most rockhounders join organized Alaska guide expeditions rather than going independently.

faq.question: Is there a permit required for rockhounding at Lituya Bay?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use recreational rockhounding at Lituya Bay. However, you should verify with Alaska Department of Natural Resources that no active mining claims exist in your intended collecting area before you visit. Respect all federal and state regulations, and pack out all waste you generate.

faq.question: What is the best time to go rockhounding at Lituya Bay?
faq.answer: June through September offers the best rockhounding conditions at Lituya Bay. Summer provides longer daylight hours, more stable weather windows, and better availability of charter boats and floatplanes from Juneau. Winter and early spring see extreme storms and cold that make access nearly impossible. Plan expeditions well in advance and monitor marine weather forecasts continuously.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding at Lituya Bay?
faq.answer: Bring a hand lens to identify jade's waxy luster and quartz crystals, heavy-duty buckets for specimens, and sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support for rocky terrain. Essential gear includes cold-weather clothing, waterproof jacket, emergency communication device (satellite messenger or marine radio), tide tables, topographic map, compass, first aid kit, and sufficient water and food. The remote location demands complete self-sufficiency.

faq.question: Is Lituya Bay a good rockhounding destination for beginners?
faq.answer: No, Lituya Bay is strictly for expert rockhounders and experienced wilderness adventurers. The extreme remoteness, dangerous glacial beach terrain, unpredictable weather, powerful tides exceeding 20 feet, cold water, wildlife hazards, and lack of services make this inappropriate for beginners or families. Only visit with experienced Alaska guides who understand the bay's specific hazards and logistics.
